input.prompt:
My MSTest project has a .testsettings file that configures test deployment, timeout,
and a code coverage data collector. After upgrading MSTest packages, the settings
seem to be ignored. How do I migrate this configuration?

data.reasoningText: The user has an MSTest project with a `.testsettings` file and wants to migrate to the modern configuration format after upgrading MSTest packages. Let me look at their project files to understand the current setup.

arguments.file_text:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<RunSettings>
  <RunConfiguration>
    <!-- 30-second timeout per test (was TestTimeout in .testsettings) -->
    <TestSessionTimeout>30000</TestSessionTimeout>
  </RunConfiguration>

  <MSTest>
    <Parallelize>
      <Scope>Method</Scope>
    </Parallelize>
    <!-- Per-test timeout; matches the old Property TestTimeout=30000 -->
    <TestTimeout>30000</TestTimeout>
    <!-- Assembly resolution with load context is the default behavior now -->
  </MSTest>

  <DataCollectionRunSettings>
    <DataCollectors>
      <DataCollector friendlyName="Code Coverage"
                     uri="datacollector://microsoft/CodeCoverage/2.0"
                     assemblyQualifiedName="Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector.CoverageCollectorDataCollector, Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a">
        <Configuration>
          <CodeCoverage />
        </Configuration>
      </DataCollector>
    </DataCollectors>
  </DataCollectionRunSettings>
</RunSettings>

data.reasoningText:
The user has upgraded to MSTest 3.8.0, which uses the modern MSTest runner. The `.testsettings` file is a legacy format that's no longer supported by the new runner. They need to migrate to a `.runsettings` file.

Let me summarize the migration:

1. **TestTimeout** → goes into `<MSTest><TestTimeout>` or `<RunConfiguration><TestTimeout>` in a `.runsettings` file
2. **Code Coverage data collector** → goes into `<DataCollectors>` in `.runsettings`
3. **Deployment/AssemblyResolution** → generally not needed with modern MSTest, but can be configured if needed


Now I'll create the `.runsettings` file with the appropriate configuration and update the project file to reference it.

arguments.file_text:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<RunSettings>
  <RunConfiguration>
    <!-- 30-second timeout per test (was TestTimeout in .testsettings) -->
    <TestSessionTimeout>30000</TestSessionTimeout>
  </RunConfiguration>

  <MSTest>
    <Parallelize>
      <Scope>Method</Scope>
    </Parallelize>
    <!-- Per-test timeout; matches the old Property TestTimeout=30000 -->
    <TestTimeout>30000</TestTimeout>
    <!-- Assembly resolution with load context is the default behavior now -->
  </MSTest>

  <DataCollectionRunSettings>
    <DataCollectors>
      <DataCollector friendlyName="Code Coverage"
                     uri="datacollector://microsoft/CodeCoverage/2.0"
                     assemblyQualifiedName="Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector.CoverageCollectorDataCollector, Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a">
        <Configuration>
          <CodeCoverage />
        </Configuration>
      </DataCollector>
    </DataCollectors>
  </DataCollectionRunSettings>
</RunSettings>

data.content: Now update the `.csproj` to reference the new runsettings:

Here's a summary of the migration:

### `.testsettings` → `.runsettings`

| Old (`.testsettings`) | New (`.runsettings`) |
|---|---|
| `<Property name="TestTimeout" value="30000" />` | `<MSTest><TestTimeout>30000</TestTimeout>` |
| `<DataCollector uri="...CodeCoverage/1.0">` | `<DataCollector uri="...CodeCoverage/2.0">` under `<DataCollectionRunSettings>` |
| `<AssemblyResolution><TestDirectory useLoadContext="true" />` | No longer needed — default behavior in modern MSTest |

**Key changes made:**

1. **Created `local.runsettings`** — the modern replacement for `.testsettings`, with your timeout and code coverage collector migrated.
2. **Updated `.csproj`** — added `<RunSettingsFilePath>` so the settings are automatically picked up by `dotnet test` and Visual Studio.

You can safely delete `local.testsettings` once you've confirmed tests run correctly. You can also pass the file explicitly: `dotnet test --settings local.runsettings`.
